paragraphs.text: 24 October 2025

section_path: Technology Ireland view on the EU Digital Fairness Act (DFA)

paragraphs.text: Ibec

Technology Ireland, the Ibec group representing the technology industry, welcomes the opportunity to share our view on the consultation on the proposed EU Digital Fairness Act (DFA). Technology Ireland has previously welcomed the European Commission's goal to prioritise simplification and coherence in EU digital regulation and to reduce burdens on all business operating in Europe; encourage innovation, boost deployment and enhance competitiveness. The DFA risks undermining this goal with new and potentially overlapping rules.

In recent years, the EU's digital regulatory landscape has grown increasingly complex, with the EU Digital Services Act (DSA), the Digital Markets Act (DMA), the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) and the AI Act, among many others. The EU must balance upholding consumer protection goals with fostering a competitive and innovative digital economy. Rather than a focus on new rules, as with the DFA, we instead encourage the EU to advance its simplification agenda and promote swift adoption and implementation of the recommendations of the Draghi Report – only $11\%$ of which have already been implemented - ensuring that regulatory frameworks in Europe foster digital innovation rather than hold it back. Accelerating these reforms can strengthen Europe's economic competitiveness and secure its leadership in the digital sphere.

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulative design
subscription traps
Personalised advertising is strictly regulated under the GDPR and DSA, requiring transparency, user choice and opt-outs.
unfair contracts

paragraphs.text: EU consumer law is principles-based and designed to adapt to new challenges without constant legislative revision. It already addresses deceptive practices, unfair contracts, manipulative design, transparency and accountability. This includes the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), the Consumer Rights Directive (CRD), the Unfair Contract Terms Directive (UCTD), and sits alongside the Digital Services Act (DSA) and the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). Common concerns, such as subscription traps, free trials or screen time management, are already regulated. For example, subscription traps are prohibited under the UCPD and CRD, while industry widely offers tools for managing screen time and spending limits. Personalised advertising is strictly regulated under the GDPR and DSA, requiring transparency, user choice and opt-outs. AI-powered chatbots in high-risk areas are covered by the AI Act, and financial services remain protected by sector-specific rights to human interaction.

evidence_quotes.quote: Influencer marketing is equally regulated through horizontal legislation, the DSA and the Audiovisual Media Services Directive (AVMSD), requiring transparency and prohibiting misleading endorsements.

paragraphs.text: Influencer marketing is equally regulated through horizontal legislation, the DSA and the Audiovisual Media Services Directive (AVMSD), requiring transparency and prohibiting misleading endorsements. Rather than layering duplicative rules that only burden compliant businesses, the EU should focus on strengthening enforcement of existing laws. Stronger oversight and enforcement will have a deterrent effect whilst supporting Europe's competitiveness agenda.

The priority of the EU simplification process must be to enable Member States and organisations to implement, coordinate and adapt to Europe's complex legislative framework so that it delivers shared digital ambitions and competitiveness. A Europe that is economically competitive is one that leads through innovation and regulating only where needed, to defend its economic achievements. EU Member States should be afforded time to enforce existing rules, many of which have only just been introduced, while balancing implementation with considerations of economic competitiveness. The DFA risks duplication of regulatory efforts on areas already within the scope of existing rules such as

evidence_quotes.quote: personalised advertising – rules in these areas are still being actively implemented across the EU.

paragraphs.text: personalised advertising – rules in these areas are still being actively implemented across the EU. Efforts should be made to ensure that any new regulation is carefully considered. Proposals for new regulation must be evaluated, targeted and developed in an evidence-based, risk-based and proportionate and prospectively avoid overlap and conflict with existing rules. Any interplay with existing rules must work with, not against, innovation and competitiveness. The focus should be on improving enforcement and raising awareness of existing rights. Strengthening the application of current law will deliver faster, more targeted benefits for consumers and businesses alike.

EU consumer protection laws have proven effective in safeguarding consumer interests by setting clear standards and providing access to transparent information and remedies. However, the rapid increase in tech-focused EU laws has created a complex and fragmented regulatory environment. This has led to a confusing patchwork of intersecting or overlapping rules across Member States, increasing complexity which can delay the launch of innovative products in the EU. Instead of introducing new, overlapping regulations, a focus on simplifying and harmonizing existing frameworks is crucial. The EU should foster a predictable and stable regulatory environment that facilitates innovation and leads to more diverse and better products and services for consumers.
Technology Ireland recommends the following:
Avoid further layers of complexity and uncertainty

Complexity and uncertainty in the EU's approach to digital rules can prevent economic activity by holding organisations back from introducing new products and services to Europe. EU policymakers need to consider the cumulative impact of the existing digital rulebook before considering any future rules on the technology sector, including the proposed DFA, with all institutions working collaboratively to undertake impact assessments and prevent unintended costs and consequences at a time when the focus in Europe needs to be on competitiveness. Policymaking and review processes should be approached first with robust evidence gathering and consultation with a broad range of stakeholders. We would encourage the completion of robust impact assessments and 'competitiveness checks' of draft proposals prior to publication.
Embrace a Risk-Based Approach

evidence_quotes.quote: A risk-based approach to regulatory simplification is key to promoting digital fairness for all consumers.

paragraphs.text: A risk-based approach to regulatory simplification is key to promoting digital fairness for all consumers. Instead of a uniform, one-size-fits-all model, focusing efforts on oversight of the most significant, high-impact risks and allowing for a more efficient use of resources. This provides predictability and certainty for companies to concentrate on developing innovative and user-friendly products. It moves the industry beyond a reactive, compliance-driven culture toward one that actively improves the online experience and encourages the creation of safer, more effective services.
Integrate Simplification from the Start

For simplification to be effective, it must be a foundational principle of good regulation, not a mere add-on. The Commission should ensure its legislative initiatives are cohesive, specifically by prioritising the Digital Simplification Omnibus over the proposed DFA. This ensures the EU

identifies opportunities to simplify the current consumer protection framework to address issues identified in the Fitness Check and avoids unnecessary new regulations which may duplicate or conflict with existing ones, ensuring any future laws are clear and impactful. This programme of work should include identifying areas where further guidance is needed to aid company compliance and whether targeted enforcement against egregious offences is merited.

evidence_quotes.quote: the EDPB guidance on dark patterns.

paragraphs.text: New legislation is only warranted if genuine regulatory gaps exist, rather than enforcement gaps. The GDPR, e-Privacy directive, DSA, DMA and the Accessibility Act, all have consumer-relevant provisions, and some of these laws have only entered into force a short time ago. Additionally, there is a body of extensive guidance - such as the EDPB guidance on dark patterns. Many of the issues the DFA is looking to address are already partially or in total covered by other laws. The proposed DFA should not add to the complexity or create overlaps or conflicts.

Much of the legislation addressing DFA topics is extremely recent and still being actively implemented. As of September 2025, the national implementation process is underway for the DSA (consultation on draft legislation published) in Poland, Spain, Czechia, Croatia, Bulgaria, Cyprus and Malta. The European Commission should therefore consider phasing its next steps to allow sufficient time to assess the impact of the DSA.
The European Commission's own Regulatory Scrutiny Board warned about "significant shortcomings" in the methodological approach and robustness of the evidence base of the DFA Fitness Check results.
Implement a Holistic Strategy

Simplification efforts should be comprehensive and not restricted to a single sector of the economy, even though supporting small and medium-sized enterprises is crucial. The digital landscape is an interconnected ecosystem, and friction at any point within the value chain negatively impacts everyone, from startups to large corporations. The goal should be to establish a coherent, trust-based framework that guarantees citizens' rights are consistently protected across the EU, while providing businesses with clear, predictable, and dependable rules.

evidence_quotes.quote: Privacy-friendly consumer laws by design

paragraphs.text: Privacy-friendly consumer laws by design

evidence_quotes.quote: identifying users that fall into those categories.

paragraphs.text: Any effort to protect specific categories of consumers should align with GDPR's data minimization principle, i.e. not require service-providers to actively collect or track additional personal data for the purpose of identifying users that fall into those categories.

evidence_quotes.quote: Protection of minors online

paragraphs.text: Protection of minors online

evidence_quotes.quote: age appropriate and safe experiences for minors

paragraphs.text: To provide age appropriate and safe experiences for minors, measures should ensure that minors receive appropriate protection, line with Article 28 of the DSA and its accompanying guidelines. Existing safeguards in the DSA and GDPR already provide robust protection, and any new obligations should avoid duplication.

evidence_quotes.quote: design of services, features and business models.

paragraphs.text: The safety and mental health of children should be considered in the design of services, features and business models.
The preferred approach is to:
Build on Article 28 of the Digital Services Act (DSA) and the Article 28 guidelines.
Balance protection with privacy and user experience considerations, ensuring that safeguards do not unintentionally limit adults' access to legitimate online content or services.

evidence_quotes.quote: Involve parents and caregivers in the design of age-appropriate experiences and consent mechanisms, empowering families to take an active role in online safety.

paragraphs.text: Involve parents and caregivers in the design of age-appropriate experiences and consent mechanisms, empowering families to take an active role in online safety.

evidence_quotes.quote: Personalisation facilitates convenient, intuitive and seamless online experiences for consumers.
Restrictions on personalisation leads to tangible economic harms for SMEs, market consolidation and higher prices.

paragraphs.text: Personalisation facilitates convenient, intuitive and seamless online experiences for consumers. Restricting it risks returning European consumers to a bygone era of the internet and wasting their valuable time and reducing the value they receive from time online. Businesses of all sizes and across all sectors rely on the digital advertising, including data-driven and personalised advertising, to promote goods and services. Digital advertising is a core commercial activity that supports the growth of small, medium and large EU businesses and powers a large proportion of the technology and media industry that invests in Ireland. Restrictions on personalisation leads to tangible economic harms for SMEs, market consolidation and higher prices. EU consumer and data protection rules govern the use of data, set rules for traders and establish a level-playing field for businesses across the supply chain which operate within the digital advertising ecosystem.

evidence_quotes.quote: The consumer experience is enhanced by ad personalisation, making adverts consumers receive more relevant.

paragraphs.text: Personalised advertising enables free access to online services for EU citizens and further restrictions will impact consumers, businesses and the economy. At a broader scale, advertising makes up around 4.6% of the EU's overall GDP. The consumer experience is enhanced by ad personalisation, making adverts consumers receive more relevant. Consumer expectations are increasingly linked to the personalisation of their ads; when ads become less personalised, consumers engage less.

evidence_quotes.quote: The proposed DFA should not introduce new rules that would prevent services from making use of personalisation to deliver higher quality, more relevant information and safer, age-appropriate online experiences.

paragraphs.text: The proposed DFA should not introduce new rules that would prevent services from making use of personalisation to deliver higher quality, more relevant information and safer, age-appropriate online experiences. Personalisation is not only valued by users to help them effectively navigate vast quantities of online content, it is also an essential tool for online services to create safer online environments, particularly for younger users.

The DSA, DMA and the AI Act entail extensive obligations and requirements on how companies develop, operate, and moderate their products. The ever-changing digital regulatory landscape creates uncertainty for businesses operating within the EU digital single market and risks a patchwork of rule-implementation.

section_path: Avoid overlapping and complex legislation which increases compliance costs and creates legal uncertainty.

paragraphs.text: Complex regulatory frameworks divert resources away from research & development towards compliance monitoring. Unnecessary DFA rules risk a departure from principle-based consumer law. Prescriptive one-size-fits-all rules are less conducive for innovation and difficult for diverse business models to integrate into their operations.

section_path: Avoid stifling innovation and the availability of innovative products

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: **Definitional issues**: The current consumer framework already provides extensive consumer protections, including via the GDPR, the AI Act (subliminal techniques), the DSA and Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD). Moreover, the EDPB has provided guidance on dark patterns, with extensive commentary on deceptive design and unlawful practices. Centralised cross-regulatory guidance on “dark patterns” which clarifies and explains the hierarchy and the interrelationship between the different regimes, including addressing the fundamental inconsistency in the use and definition. An ongoing dialogue between policymakers, influencers, online platforms and other stakeholders would ensure regulatory frameworks are informed by practical insights and experiences.

evidence_quotes.quote: deceptive design

paragraphs.text: EU consumer law such as the Unfair Commercial Practices Directive (UCPD), Digital Services Act (DSA), AI Act and the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) contain relevant provisions to tackle deceptive design, for example:

evidence_quotes.quote: subliminal or purposefully manipulative or deceptive techniques

paragraphs.text: Article 5(1) AI Act expressly prohibits practices related to AI that deploy subliminal or purposefully manipulative or deceptive techniques, with the objective or effect of materially distorting the behaviour of a person by impairing the ability to make an informed decision and causing them to take a decision that they would not otherwise have taken.

evidence_quotes.quote: Article 25 DSA expressly prohibits the design of an online interface "in a way that deceives or manipulates the recipients" of a service or "in a way that otherwise materially distorts or impairs" the ability of users "to make free and informed decisions".

paragraphs.text: Article 25 DSA expressly prohibits the design of an online interface "in a way that deceives or manipulates the recipients" of a service or "in a way that otherwise materially distorts or impairs" the ability of users "to make free and informed decisions".

evidence_quotes.quote: "overall presentation" of a commercial practice being capable of constituting a misleading action if it "deceives or is likely to deceive the average consumer, even if the information is factually correct"

paragraphs.text: Article 6(1) UCPD explicitly calls out the "overall presentation" of a commercial practice being capable of constituting a misleading action if it "deceives or is likely to deceive the average consumer, even if the information is factually correct", and the practice causes or is likely to cause the average consumer to take a transactional decision they would not otherwise have taken in relation to certain mandatory consumer law obligations.

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: The primary focus should be on consistent enforcement of these existing laws. The proposed DFA should avoid introducing unnecessary duplication of existing legislation by focusing on specific gaps, if they exist, and encourage consumer awareness and education efforts to tackle the issue of dark patterns.

evidence_quotes.quote: decisions have to be made regarding the form and manner in which information is presented.

paragraphs.text: Consumer-centric design: Online services are best placed to design their services in a consumer-centric way, giving consumers a seamless experience which empowers consumer choice, including regarding the use of their personal data. With this in mind, it is important to emphasise that, in an online context, and particularly for mobile devices, where space is, by definition, constrained, decisions have to be made regarding the form and manner in which information is presented.

evidence_quotes.quote: (subscriptions, free trials, etc.)

paragraphs.text: The EU’s diverse digital economy thrives on various monetisation models (subscriptions, free trials, etc.). As long as consumer laws are met, the EU should not limit this variety for consumers. There is already a robust regulatory framework

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

evidence_quotes.quote: pre- and post-contractual information obligations.

paragraphs.text: governing digital contracts and consumers are comprehensively protected through an extensive framework of pre- and post-contractual information obligations. The DFA should focus on specific needs that aren't covered in the current playbook, while considering the needed balance between consumer's and trader's interests.

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

evidence_quotes.quote: If the EU were to mandate a second payment process at the end of the free trial this would be unnecessarily burdensome for consumers and dissuade many developers from offering free trials - effectively undercutting one important commercial avenue for small developers.
Respective implementation burdens would likely discourage particularly smaller developers to offer free trials at all or lead to an increase in subscription prices to the detriment of consumers.
Consumers may also be confused, thinking they are being asked to pay again.

paragraphs.text: **Free trials:** Many online service providers choose the option to offer free trials as a pathway to go to market and make their users a unique offering. As the name suggests, these are trials - not free services - so it is natural that a payment process is initiated at the start of the free trial. This is also necessary for other important reasons: To verify the age of the user, to prevent abuse and fraud (for instance, using multiple accounts to repeat-use free trials). If the EU were to mandate a second payment process at the end of the free trial this would be unnecessarily burdensome for consumers and dissuade many developers from offering free trials - effectively undercutting one important commercial avenue for small developers. Consumers may also be confused, thinking they are being asked to pay again. Respective implementation burdens would likely discourage particularly smaller developers to offer free trials at all or lead to an increase in subscription prices to the detriment of consumers.

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

evidence_quotes.quote: Consumers already consent to renewals and can manage reminders and cancellations at any time.
Mandated, frequent reminders would overwhelm users ('reminder fatigue') and take away their control over notification preferences.
Consumers should be empowered to choose the nature/frequency of reminders for themselves.

paragraphs.text: **Automatic renewals & reminders:** Consumers already consent to renewals and can manage reminders and cancellations at any time. Mandated, frequent reminders would overwhelm users ('reminder fatigue') and take away their control over notification preferences. Consumers should be empowered to choose the nature/frequency of reminders for themselves.

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

evidence_quotes.quote: subscriptions can be cancelled at any time with short notice
explicit consent for every subscription renewal or free trial conversion

paragraphs.text: **Subscription cancellations:** The proposed DFA should refrain from requiring that subscriptions can be cancelled at any time with short notice, as it could discourage businesses from offering discounted long-term subscriptions and consequently limit the consumer's options. Moreover, it shouldn't mandate explicit consent for every subscription renewal or free trial conversion, as this could be burdensome and irritating for users, especially for short term subscriptions.

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

evidence_quotes.quote: Mandating a specific, one-click 'cancellation button' is not evidence-based.

paragraphs.text: **Cancellation button:** EU law already requires easy cancellation. Mandating a specific, one-click 'cancellation button' is not evidence-based. This idea denies providers the ability to authenticate a cancellation request first and opens services to abuse and fraud. Such an overly prescriptive design choice that ignores platform diversity (e.g. centralised platform management) and could confuse users. In the context of a phone screen (given the limited real space, for instance) it would be technically challenging to have a long list of cancellation buttons that do not confuse customers or lead to accidental cancellations. Moreover, mandating a specific product design prevents innovation in this field in the future. One day there could be better methods in future than a simple button, such as new UX flows.

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

paragraphs.text: **On 'Section 8 - Simplification Measures. Question 4':**

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

paragraphs.text: As noted above, opportunities for simplification of the consumer acquis should be explored under initiatives already underway and target accordingly via omnibus proposals. Simplification should not be used to justify broad new legislation under the DFA.

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

paragraphs.text: **Digitisation of consumer information:**

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

paragraphs.text: Further progress with the digitisation of consumer information could be explored. Providing information to consumers in digital form allows consumers to receive more relevant information, information that is up to date, available in more languages, reduces costs and operational burden as well as preventing environmental waste.

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

paragraphs.text: **On specific features in digital products:**

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

evidence_quotes.quote: gaming

paragraphs.text: Simplification should deliver a technology-neutral and risk-based approach, focusing on specific consumer needs and avoiding duplication of the current legislation covering specific features in gaming and digital products, such as the UCPD and the Consumer Rights Directive (CRD):

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

evidence_quotes.quote: When a business offers a virtual currency, it must follow the UCPD's information rules and not mislead customers.

paragraphs.text: When a business offers a virtual currency, it must follow the UCPD's information rules and not mislead customers.

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

evidence_quotes.quote: gaming

paragraphs.text: The Commission’s UCPD guidance includes a section on gaming, which covers loot boxes, among other issues.

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

evidence_quotes.quote: in-app purchases made with real money (including vouchers or gift cards).

paragraphs.text: The CRD pre- and post-contractual information obligations and other consumer protections cover in-app purchases made with real money (including vouchers or gift cards).

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

evidence_quotes.quote: uncertainty-based rewards in games.

paragraphs.text: Some aspects of these features are also regulated by national laws, particularly in relation to uncertainty-based rewards in games.

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

evidence_quotes.quote: In-game virtual items (including so-called in-game “currencies”) have been classified as digital content under the Consumer Rights Directive and the Digital Content Directive and as confirmed by case law, exchanges of in-game “currencies” for other virtual items in a game are not purchases under EU consumer law.
in-game currencies cannot simply be removed from a game or service.

paragraphs.text: In-game virtual items (including so-called in-game “currencies”) have been classified as digital content under the Consumer Rights Directive and the Digital Content Directive and as confirmed by case law, exchanges of in-game “currencies” for other virtual items in a game are not purchases under EU consumer law. As such, these in-game items are not comparable to real world currencies and cannot be considered a payment method. Because of this, requiring providers to display a monetary value at all times and/or maintaining an exchange rate system would fuel fraud and create the misconception that the in-game content has actual financial value and can be exchanged for real money. Moreover, we must point out that features such as in-game currencies cannot simply be removed from a game or service. They are complex mechanisms that are integrated into the design and switching them on or off would require material investment in design, including creating multiple versions of the service or content, in turn increasing required investment and complexity in operation. For many, such a requirement would not be possible to implement in practice or from an economic perspective, threatening the availability of a very significant section of the EU market.

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

evidence_quotes.quote: Many digital games offer intermediate virtual currency that players can earn through gameplay or purchase at various rates (e.g. due to bundle deals).

paragraphs.text: Many digital games offer intermediate virtual currency that players can earn through gameplay or purchase at various rates (e.g. due to bundle deals). This makes it challenging for publishers to assign a single, universal conversion rate to the virtual

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

evidence_quotes.quote: Mandating the display of a real-world currency equivalent for every exchange with in-game items

paragraphs.text: currency. Mandating the display of a real-world currency equivalent for every exchange with in-game items could potentially confuse consumers through indication of inaccurate values and / or require businesses to process even more personal data to ensure accuracy.

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

paragraphs.text: Simplification should encourage effective transparency about potential risks and the empowerment of consumers' education and controls tailored to specific services, while considering industry best practices.

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

paragraphs.text: Protecting minors

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

evidence_quotes.quote: alternative recommender systems that are not based on profiling

paragraphs.text: While more needs to be done to protect minors online, the proposed DFA is not the correct instrument. The DSA provides a strong regulatory framework to address concerns related to minors' well-being online. The DSA also mandates that platforms offer alternative recommender systems that are not based on profiling, giving users greater control over their online experiences.

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

paragraphs.text: It has been argued that the DFA will fill gaps, but this may be premature given continued advancements with both the DSA and the forthcoming European Commission age verification app. Effective child protection requires a risk-based approach to age verification and assurance solutions, like the one championed in DSA Article 28. This measured approach is crucial for providing necessary protections while preserving children's access to valuable digital content.

section_path: On Section 7 - Issues with digital contracts:

paragraphs.text: The EU already boasts one of the world's most comprehensive consumer protection frameworks, applying horizontally across all consumer-facing businesses. The proposed DFA would merely duplicate existing regulations, driven by the mistaken belief that technology companies require special regulation. Instead, the EU must prioritise the implementation and oversight of its existing consumer and digital laws before adding new regulatory layers that wouldn't bring any substantial benefits and harm economic development. The European Commission should refocus its priorities on an updated oversight strategy – improving guidance to aid compliance, regulatory coordination, resource allocation, information sharing and the use of technology investigations. It should also recognise the role of public-private partnerships and promote greater cooperation with industry.
The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
